title: OAuth2 security schemes duplicated in OpenAPI, with and without scopes, when used at the router level
body:
### Privileged issue

- [x] I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

### Issue Content

OAuth2 security schemes duplicated in OpenAPI, with and without scopes, when used at the router level

Reported by @Kludex on another channel 🙌 

(I'm working on it)

```Python
# /// script
# dependencies = [
#   "fastapi[standard]==0.121.3",
# ]
# ///

import json
import sys
from typing import Annotated

import fastapi
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, FastAPI, Security
from fastapi.security import OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer, SecurityScopes


# print the dependencies
print(f'fastapi version = {fastapi.__version__}')
print(f'Python version = {sys.version}')



app = FastAPI()

oauth2_scheme = OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer(
    authorizationUrl='api/oauth/authorize',
    tokenUrl='/api/oauth/token',
    refreshUrl='/api/oauth/token',
    auto_error=False,
    scopes={'read': 'Read access', 'write': 'Write access'},
)

async def get_token(
    token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2_scheme)],
) -> str:
    return token

AccessToken = Annotated[str, Depends(get_token, use_cache=True)]


async def require_oauth_scopes(security_scopes: SecurityScopes, token: AccessToken) -> None:
    pass        


async def check_limit(token: AccessToken) -> None:
    pass

# The problem is here. If we remove the dependency from the router, the schema is generated correctly in both fastapi versions.
router = APIRouter(prefix='/v1', dependencies=[Depends(check_limit)])

channels_router = APIRouter(prefix='/channels', tags=['Channels'])
@channels_router.get("/", dependencies=[Security(require_oauth_scopes, scopes=['read'])])
def read_items():
    return {"msg": "You have READ access"}


router.include_router(channels_router)

app.include_router(router)
schema = app.openapi()
pretty_schema = json.dumps(schema, indent=4)
print(pretty_schema)

"""
with fastapi 0.123.7, the generated openapi schema icludes security without scopes:

                "security": [
                    {
                        "OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer": []
                    }
                ]

but in fastapi 0.121.3, the generated openapi schema includes two items in security:

                "security": [
                    {
                        "OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer": []
                    },
                    {
                        "OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer": [
                            "read"
                        ]
                    }
                ]
"""
```
comments: This was fixed by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14455, released in FastAPI 0.123.8 :tada:

That didn't fix it. 😱 

Working on it now.
This should be fixed now in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14459 (strike 2 ⚾). Released in FastAPI 0.123.9 🚀

title: FastAPI 0.123.5 breaks async wrappers using @wraps
body:
(Already being worked on) ### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/14442
Originally posted by **falkoschindler** December 3, 2025 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from functools import wraps import uvicorn from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() def my_decorator(func): """A decorator that wraps a sync function with an async handler.""" @wraps(func) async def wrapper(): func() return 'OK' return wrapper @app.get('/') @my_decorator def index(): """A simple sync page function.""" print('Hello!') if __name__ == '__main__': uvicorn.run(app) ``` ### Description * Visit "/". * You'll see "Internal Server Error". * A ValueError appears: ``` ValueError: [TypeError("'coroutine' object is not iterable"), TypeError('vars() argument must have __dict__ attribute')] ``` Since FastAPI 0.123.5 (PR #14434), using `functools.wraps` on an async wrapper around a sync function causes FastAPI to incorrectly treat the handler as sync, resulting in: `ValueError: [TypeError("'coroutine' object is not iterable"), ...]` The issue is that `Dependant.is_coroutine_callable` now uses `inspect.unwrap()` which follows `__wrapped__` back to the original sync function, instead of checking if the actual registered handler is async. ### Operating System macOS ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.123.5 ### Pydantic Version 2.12.5 ### Python Version 3.11.11 ### Additional Context _No response_
comments:
This should be fixed by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14448

It's now released in FastAPI 0.123.6 :tada:

title: Regression between 0.113.0 and 0.114.0: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm used to accept grant_type=""
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12182
Originally posted by **rbubley** September 10, 2024 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python # This is just the code from https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/#update-the-dependencies from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone from typing import Annotated import jwt from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer, OAuth2PasswordRequestForm from jwt.exceptions import InvalidTokenError from passlib.context import CryptContext from pydantic import BaseModel # to get a string like this run: # openssl rand -hex 32 SECRET_KEY = "09d25e094faa6ca2556c818166b7a9563b93f7099f6f0f4caa6cf63b88e8d3e7" ALGORITHM = "HS256" ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES = 30 fake_users_db = { "johndoe": { "username": "johndoe", "full_name": "John Doe", "email": "johndoe@example.com", "hashed_password": "$2b$12$EixZaYVK1fsbw1ZfbX3OXePaWxn96p36WQoeG6Lruj3vjPGga31lW", "disabled": False, } } class Token(BaseModel): access_token: str token_type: str class TokenData(BaseModel): username: str | None = None class User(BaseModel): username: str email: str | None = None full_name: str | None = None disabled: bool | None = None class UserInDB(User): hashed_password: str pwd_context = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto") oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="token") app = FastAPI() def verify_password(plain_password, hashed_password): return pwd_context.verify(plain_password, hashed_password) def get_password_hash(password): return pwd_context.hash(password) def get_user(db, username: str): if username in db: user_dict = db[username] return UserInDB(**user_dict) def authenticate_user(fake_db, username: str, password: str): user = get_user(fake_db, username) if not user: return False if not verify_password(password, user.hashed_password): return False return user def create_access_token(data: dict, expires_delta: timedelta | None = None): to_encode = data.copy() if expires_delta: expire = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + expires_delta else: expire = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(minutes=15) to_encode.update({"exp": expire}) encoded_jwt = jwt.encode(to_encode, SECRET_KEY, algorithm=ALGORITHM) return encoded_jwt async def get_current_user(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2_scheme)]): credentials_exception = HTTPException( status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="Could not validate credentials", headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"}, ) try: payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=[ALGORITHM]) username: str = payload.get("sub") if username is None: raise credentials_exception token_data = TokenData(username=username) except InvalidTokenError: raise credentials_exception user = get_user(fake_users_db, username=token_data.username) if user is None: raise credentials_exception return user async def get_current_active_user( current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_user)], ): if current_user.disabled: raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Inactive user") return current_user @app.post("/token") async def login_for_access_token( form_data: Annotated[OAuth2PasswordRequestForm, Depends()], ) -> Token: user = authenticate_user(fake_users_db, form_data.username, form_data.password) if not user: raise HTTPException( status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="Incorrect username or password", headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"}, ) access_token_expires = timedelta(minutes=ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES) access_token = create_access_token( data={"sub": user.username}, expires_delta=access_token_expires ) return Token(access_token=access_token, token_type="bearer") @app.get("/users/me/", response_model=User) async def read_users_me( current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_active_user)], ): return current_user @app.get("/users/me/items/") async def read_own_items( current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_active_user)], ): return [{"item_id": "Foo", "owner": current_user.username}] ``` ### Description * Open the browser, and navigate to to /token endpoint under the swagger interface. * delete "password" from grant_access, to leave an empty string * Put in the usual `johndoe` and `secret` and username and password. Under 0.113.0 this worked and returned a token. Under 0.114.0 it returns an error message: a 422 error, and "String should match pattern 'password'" I expect it to work, because the docs for OAuth2PasswordRequestForm say (/fastapi/security/outh2.py lines 69-72): ``` The OAuth2 spec says it is required and MUST be the fixed string "password". Nevertheless, this dependency class is permissive and allows not passing it. If you want to enforce it, use instead the `OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict` dependency. ``` ### Operating System macOS ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.114.0 ### Pydantic Version 2.8.2 ### Python Version 3.12.5 ### Additional Context _No response_
comments:
@Kludex I don't know if this is the correct way, but we can change [this code](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/security/oauth2.py#L64-L75) by replacing the form pattern with the default value. Something like this

```python
grant_type: Annotated[
    Union[str, None],
    Form(),
    Doc(
        """
        The OAuth2 spec says it is required and MUST be the fixed string
        "password". Nevertheless, this dependency class is permissive and
        allows not passing it. If you want to enforce it, use instead the
        `OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict` dependency.
        """
    ),
] = "password",
```

After that change, everything works with the default or empty value.
Is there any objection to this proposed fix?

Investigating this report, I was able to trace the origin of the problem and opened a new discussion here https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12536 as it broadly concerns Forms.

I can confirm and have verified that the issue exists and that the proposed solution could be applied to mitigate the problem. However, I do not agree with accepting this solution as a fix for two reasons:

1. The cause of the issue is not within the OAuth2PasswordRequestForm class.
2. The relaxed value of that field is fixed to two possible values: password, which is specific to OAuth, and None, which constitutes the relaxed part but is easily associated with the name in the class. 
In the proposed solution, however, the grant_type field could be associated with any value.
This was fixed by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13537 and available in FastAPI 0.123.2 🎉

title: functools.partial() does not work on an async dependable
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9744
Originally posted by **lieryan** June 26, 2023 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from functools import partial from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends app = FastAPI() def the_answer(): return 42 async def dep1( n: int = Depends(the_answer), ): return n + 1 @app.get("/") def main( d1=Depends(partial(dep1, n=10)), ): print(d1) # prints "" return {} if __name__ == '__main__': import uvicorn uvicorn.run(app, port=1122) ``` ### Description I would've expected `d1` above to be the return value of the dependable, but it seems like fastapi didn't identify the partial as an coroutine function and instead passed the unawaited coroutine into the view function. `inspect.iscoroutinefunction()` and `inspect.signature()` were able to deconstruct the partial object to correctly identify whether a partial contains a coroutine or a regular function, so it seems like it should've been possible for FastAPI to use handle partial correctly: ``` In [5]: def regular(a, b): pass In [6]: async def coro(a, b): pass In [7]: inspect.iscoroutinefunction(partial(regular, 1)) Out[7]: False In [8]: inspect.iscoroutinefunction(partial(coro, 1)) Out[8]: True In [20]: inspect.signature(partial(coro, 1)) Out[20]: ``` ### Operating System Linux ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.98.0 ### Python Version Python 3.10.6 ### Additional Context _No response_
comments: I understand that using an asynchronous function without `await` in a synchronous function will naturally result in a coroutine object. Could you please clarify the intention behind your question? It seems evident that this behavior is expected in Python's asynchronous programming model.

> I understand that using an asynchronous function without `await` in a synchronous function will naturally result in a coroutine object. Could you please clarify the intention behind your question? It seems evident that this behavior is expected in Python's asynchronous programming model.


In my opinion,in FastAPI, dependency functions are automatically executed with await added.
This is based on the [documentation](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/#to-async-or-not-to-async)

There is a pull request that has the test cases and the fix for this issue: #9753.

@atapin yeah, async generator dependency works, but it doesn't work for async regular function dependency.
This should be solved by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/9753, included in FastAPI 0.123.5, released in the next few hours. Thanks @lieryan! 🎉

title: [BUG] In version 0.115.0 of FastAPI, the pydantic model that has declared an alias cannot correctly receive query parameters
body:
Thank you for all the work you have done. I have initiated the [discussion ](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12401)as requested, but I think this issue is quite important. Initiating this issue is just to prevent the discussion from being drowned out, and I apologize for any offense.

### Example Code

```python
import uvicorn
from typing import Literal

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field
from pydantic.alias_generators import to_camel

app = FastAPI()


class FilterParams(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(alias_generator=to_camel)

    limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
    offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
    order_by: Literal['created_at', 'updated_at'] = 'created_at'
    tags: list[str] = []


@app.get('/items/')
async def read_items(filter_query: FilterParams = Query()):
    return filter_query


if __name__ == '__main__':
    uvicorn.run(app='app:app')
```


### Description

Running the code in the example above, I encountered an incorrect result when accessing http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?offset=1&orderBy=updated_at in the browser, orderBy did not receive successfully.
```
{
    "limit": 100,
    "offset": 1,
    "orderBy": "created_at",
    "tags": []
}
```
The correct result should be as follows
```
{
    "limit": 100,
    "offset": 1,
    "orderBy": "updated_at",
    "tags": []
}
```

### Operating System

Windows

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.115.0

### Pydantic Version

2.9.2

### Python Version

3.9.19

### Additional Context

_No response_
comments:
Did you try with the `Annotated` version as in the docs?
```
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing_extensions import Annotated, Literal

app = FastAPI()


class FilterParams(BaseModel):
    limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
    offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
    order_by: Literal["created_at", "updated_at"] = "created_at"
    tags: list[str] = []


@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
    return filter_query
```

Hello everyone! Can you confirm that it is not possible to have other query params along with the pydantic model ?
```
from typing import Annotated, Literal

import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

app = FastAPI()


class FilterParams(BaseModel):
    limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
    offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
    order_by: Literal["created_at", "updated_at"] = "created_at"
    tags: list[str] = []


@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(
        filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()],
        another_param: Annotated[int, Query()]
):
    print(filter_query, another_param)
```

The generated swagger looks like a bit messy in this case 
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/22127fd4-cc7a-4dd0-807c-0c47a6416b9c)

> Hello everyone! Can you confirm that it is not possible to have other query params along with the pydantic model ?
> 
> ```
> from typing import Annotated, Literal
> 
> import uvicorn
> from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
> from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
> 
> app = FastAPI()
> 
> 
> class FilterParams(BaseModel):
>     limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
>     offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
>     order_by: Literal["created_at", "updated_at"] = "created_at"
>     tags: list[str] = []
> 
> 
> @app.get("/items/")
> async def read_items(
>         filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()],
>         another_param: Annotated[int, Query()]
> ):
>     print(filter_query, another_param)
> ```
> 
> The generated swagger looks like a bit messy in this case ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/40054825/374874000-22127fd4-cc7a-4dd0-807c-0c47a6416b9c.png?jwt=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..a8ylWR2Yxs7BmYMckXcNlBRnNlhgAjFIqssvsHtS4Gs)

Yes I believe you cannot use both Pydantic Models and regular Query parameters. You have to pick one.
Refs: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12379 https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12236 https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12212

> Did you try with the `Annotated` version as in the docs?
> ```
> from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
> from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
> from typing_extensions import Annotated, Literal
> 
> app = FastAPI()
> 
> 
> class FilterParams(BaseModel):
>     limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
>     offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
>     order_by: Literal["created_at", "updated_at"] = "created_at"
>     tags: list[str] = []
> 
> 
> @app.get("/items/")
> async def read_items(filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
>     return filter_query
> ```

Hello, Same issue here. I also tried the annotated version, there is no difference. (And I used a simple alias like `Field(None, alias='test')` instead of an alias generator.)

Hi, given what I see in the docs screenshot above, this is the same issue I am facing with Header parameter models.
fastapi==0.115.2

To be clear this does NOT happen with the example in the docs, which is likely the only case that was tested (I don't intend to be rude, this is just an observation)

Given this code:

```python
class CommonHeaders(BaseModel):
    host: str
class CommonHeaders2(BaseModel):
    host2: str

@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(headers: Annotated[CommonHeaders, Header()],):
                     #headers2: Annotated[CommonHeaders2, Header()]):
    return {}
```
We get image 1: 
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/05a56fac-0bea-4168-853e-1d9af1aeb732)

When we uncomment the headers2 we get image 2:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/caf8534f-a8e2-4e04-9f58-deb4689217f4)

Although the origin of these issues can be the same, there are two problems here. This issue is about the alias handling in query parameter models. @Usernamelesss introduced another problem (using multiple query parameter models together), and @Kyle-sandeman-mrdfood 's comment with header parameter models seems very similar to that. @jd-solanki linked question #12212 about this topic. I think, a proper issue should be converted from #12212 as well.

I'm having the same issue on `fastapi==0.115.6`

As a workaround, I've been able to combine a pydantic model + extra query params by abusing `fastapi.Depends`:

```python
from typing import Annotated

import fastapi
import pydantic

app = fastapi.FastAPI()


class FilterParams(pydantic.BaseModel):
    limit: int
    offset: int


@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(
    filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, fastapi.Depends(FilterParams)],
    serial_number: Annotated[str, fastapi.Query(alias="serial-number")],
):
    return {}
```

I.e we can aggregate several query parameters using `Depends` + some callable. In this case, the callable happens to be a pydantic model constructor.
https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/#create-a-dependency-or-dependable

This seems to handle validation errors as expected.

The only remaining issue is that aliases are still not properly handled if they're part of the pydantic model.
But at least we can have those as independent arguments, which is not possible when annotating the model with `Query()`.

Interestingly, this also works:
```python
class FilterParams(pydantic.BaseModel):
    serial_number: str = pydantic.Field(alias="alias")


@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(
    filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, fastapi.Depends(FilterParams)],
```

But it doesn't work if the alias is not a valid python identifier, like `serial-number` in my first example (because of the hyphen `-`).

why ?: because pydantic changes the callable signature if the alias is a valid python identifier, but it doesn't otherwise:
```python
import inspect

class FilterParams(pydantic.BaseModel):
    serial_number: str = pydantic.Field(alias="alias")

inspect.signature(FilterParams)
> <Signature (*, alias: str) -> None>

class FilterParams(pydantic.BaseModel):
    serial_number: str = pydantic.Field(alias="serial-number")

inspect.signature(FilterParams)
> <Signature (*, serial_number: str) -> None>
```
So when `fastapi.Depends` inspects the callable, it doesn't see the alias if it isn't a valid python identifier.
Why haven't the changes been committed yet😂

🚫 Query() with a BaseModel: Not Allowed

```python
@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(filter_query: FilterParams = Query()):
    ...
```

- Query() is used to declare individual query parameters.
- You can’t use Query() to inject an entire Pydantic model.
- This will raise an error: FastAPI expects a plain type (like str, int, etc.), not a Pydantic model.

---

✅ Depends() with a BaseModel: The Correct Way

```python
@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(filter_query: FilterParams = Depends()):
    ...
```

- Depends() is FastAPI’s way of injecting dependencies.
- FastAPI reads the query parameters and populates the Pydantic model for you, field by field.
- It’s how you cleanly parse groups of query params into structured data.

---

Think of it this way:
- Query(), Path(), Body() — these are field-level declarations.
- Depends() — this is function-level dependency injection, and that’s how FastAPI knows to “build” the whole FilterParams object from query parameters.

⸻

💡 Visual Analogy

```python
# This:
@app.get("/search/")
async def search_items(limit: int = Query(10), offset: int = Query(0)):
    ...

# Is equivalent to:
class SearchParams(BaseModel):
    limit: int = 10
    offset: int = 0
```

Using a model is cleaner when you’re dealing with multiple parameters that logically belong together (like filtering or pagination).

thank you for pointing out the `Depends()`-Syntax to use with pydantic-Models @JobyGeJo . I did not get this possibility from the documentation https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/ which talks only about functions for `Depends(...)`.

When I use this `Depends()`-syntax, aliases in models are working for me (using `Annotaded` + `Query()` Syntax the paramter `test_paramter` in the following example would not work at all) BUT ~~field_validators are not working anymore... `v` will be an empty object, regardless of `another_test_parameter`'s value (which again works using  `Annotaded` + `Query()` Syntax...)~~ 
- field_validators behave different (raised `ValueError` will be handled differently)
- list-parameters do not work: they will not be part of the query but of the request-body

```python
from typing import Annotated, Any
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class FilterParams(BaseModel):
    test_parameter: str = Field(alias='testParameter')
    another_test_parameter: str | None = None
    list_test_parameter: list[str] | None = None # will not be part of query, but of request-body when using Depends()

    @field_validator("another_test_parameter", mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def ensure_foobar(cls, v: Any):
        if 'foobar' not in v:
            raise ValueError('"foobar" not found in another_test_parameter')
        return v

app = FastAPI()

# alias does work, list_test_parameter is not part of the filter_query
@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(filter_query: FilterParams = Depends()):
   return filter_query

# alias does not work, list_test_parameter IS part of the filter_query
@app.get("/items2/")
async def read_items2(filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
   return filter_query
```

unfortunately I did not find a way to get both (aliases and list-fields in query) working together...

EDIT: sorry, cannot reproduce it anymore... it seems the `Depends()`-Syntax is also working for `field_validator`, but only the `ValueError` is handled less elegant by default....
EDIT2: ok, I figured it out: the issue with the `Depends()`-syntax is more about `list`-Parameters than about `field_validator`

Glad it helped @jannis701, and thanks for sharing your findings! Yeah, FastAPI treats ```Depends()``` differently because it expects it to load models from the body by default (which explains why lists go to the body, not the query). For query parameters, ```Annotated[Model, Depends(Query(...))]``` or other custom handling might be needed to get full control over both aliasing and list parsing.

following the Documentation at https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-param-models/ I would expect that the `Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]`-Syntax should work, so I would still consider it a bug, that aliases of Pydantic-Fields are broken with this syntax. I found this workaround, but I don't like it, as I would have to include FastApi-Query-Functions in my Pydantic-Models:

```python
from typing import Annotated
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class FilterParams(BaseModel):
    test_parameter_pydantic: str = Field(alias='testParameterPydantic') # does not work, I consider this a bug
    test_parameter: str = Query(alias='testParameter') # WORKAROUND: use Query instead of Field
    another_test_parameter: str | None = None
    list_test_parameter: list[str] | None = None

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items2/")
async def read_items2(filter_query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
   return filter_query
```

You're absolutely right in showing that when using `Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]`, the `alias` defined via `Field(...)` is not respected, while it works as expected when using `Depends()`. This reinforces the point that FastAPI handles Pydantic models differently depending on whether you're using `Depends()` or `Query()`.

Also, a small clarification: even with `Depends()`, `list_test_parameter` *is* parsed from the query parameters on `GET` requests — it's not treated as part of the body unless you're using `POST` or explicitly define it as such. So the issue really comes down to the alias support, not the data location in this case.

I agree this feels like inconsistent behavior, and I still consider it a bug in how `Query()` interacts with Pydantic models and field aliases. For now, I’ll stick with using `Depends()` since it preserves alias mappings cleanly and keeps the query parsing behavior intuitive.

It seems to me, that this bug has been fixed here: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/commit/88d4f2cb1814392f54011b2bbd3fe55c5f2a3278 but this commit re-introduced it: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/commit/55035f440bf852f739e3ccd71b67034016ae9bba#diff-aef3dac481b68359f4edd6974fa3a047cfde595254a4567a560cebc9ccb0673fR765

For me changing [this Line](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L783) to     

```python
values[field.alias] = value
```

(as it was done by the first mentioned commit) makes everything work as expected using the `Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]`-syntax
It seems this was solved in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14360, available in FastAPI 0.123.3 🎉

title: OpenAPI servers not being returned according how the docs say they should be
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12226
Originally posted by **mzealey** September 19, 2024 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() # you can add a test endpoint here or not - same bug either way ``` ### Description ``` $ curl localhost:8$ curl localhost:8000/openapi.json {"openapi":"3.1.0","info":{"title":"FastAPI","version":"0.1.0"},"paths":{}} ``` According to the documentation of the `servers` parameter in FastAPI: > If the servers list is not provided, or is an empty list, the default value would be a dict with a url value of /. (assuming that `root_path_in_servers = True` (the default)) Clearly this is not happening. ### Operating System Linux ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.110.3 (but according to github code seems to be in latest also) ### Pydantic Version 2.5.3 ### Python Version Python 3.10.12 ### Additional Context _No response_
comments: Relevant part of the docstrings: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/9606b916ef7c883f5ebeac1bf3db9adf5ae646a9/fastapi/applications.py#L299-L300

@Kludex I don't know if this is the correct way, but we can add default value of `[{"url": "/"}]` instead of `None`. Something like this
```python
servers: Annotated[
    Optional[List[Dict[str, Union[str, Any]]]],
    Doc(
        """
        # unchanged docstring
        """
    ),
] = [{"url": "/"}],
```
Result for default:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
```
is 
```bash
> curl localhost:8000/openapi.json
{"openapi":"3.1.0","info":{"title":"FastAPI","version":"0.1.0"},"servers":[{"url":"/"}],"paths":{}}
```
Result for provided urls:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(
    servers=[
        {"url": "https://stag.example.com", "description": "Staging environment"},
        {"url": "https://prod.example.com", "description": "Production environment"},
    ]
)
```
is

```bash
> curl localhost:8000/openapi.json
{"openapi":"3.1.0","info":{"title":"FastAPI","version":"0.1.0"},"servers":[{"url":"https://stag.example.com","description":"Staging environment"},{"url":"https://prod.example.com","description":"Production environment"}],"paths":{}}
```

Interestingly I see at https://swagger.io/docs/specification/api-host-and-base-path/:

> If the servers array is not provided or is empty, the server URL defaults to /:

But a lot of readers don't seem to do this so probably worth fully specifying it in FastAPI.

> Relevant part of the docstrings:
> 
> [fastapi/fastapi/applications.py](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/9606b916ef7c883f5ebeac1bf3db9adf5ae646a9/fastapi/applications.py#L299-L300)
> 
> Lines 299 to 300 in [9606b91](/fastapi/fastapi/commit/9606b916ef7c883f5ebeac1bf3db9adf5ae646a9)
> 
>                  If the servers `list` is not provided, or is an empty `list`, the 
>                  default value would be a `dict` with a `url` value of `/`.

I think that current docstring is not entirely correct. It will default to mounting point of the app, not just `'/'`.

And, it does work correctly for sub-applications:

```py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
sub = FastAPI()

app.mount("/sub", sub)
```

**http://localhost:8000/sub/openapi.json**

```json
{
  "openapi": "3.1.0",
  "info": {
    "title": "FastAPI",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "servers": [
    {
      "url": "/sub"  // Not just `/`, but mounting point of sub-app
    }
  ],
  "paths": {
  }
}
```

And with `root_path` as well:

```py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(root_path="/api")
```

**http://localhost:8000/api/openapi.json**

```json
{
  "openapi": "3.1.0",
  "info": {
    "title": "FastAPI",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "servers": [
    {
      "url": "/api"  // Not just `/`, but mounting point of app (root_path)
    }
  ],
  "paths": {

  }
}
```

At the same time, `servers` is [optional according to openapi spec](url). So, it's ok to omit it if the mounting point is `'/'`.

So, I think everything works correctly and we just need to fix docstring to something like:
```
                If the servers `list` is not provided, or is an empty `list`, the
                default value would be a `dict` with a `url` value of mounting point of this application.
```

We can probably explain this in more details in docs and add a link to this docstring
This should be solved by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14405, thanks all! 🍰

title: Multiple regressions in the handling of forms & form validation
body:
### Issue Content

We have found at least 2 regression in the way forms are handled while trying to migrate to v0.115.11 from version v0.112.4. After investigation we were able to identify 2 commits that introduce 2 separate bugs that break the handling of default values in both `x-form-urlencoded` and `mutlipart` froms. Partial fixes have been proposed that also address a CVE, but the PR has been hanging since october 24.

# Breaking in the handling of default value in `x-url-encoded` forms

This bug has been introduced in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12134.

MRE:
```python
app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/")
def root(
    name: Annotated[Optional[str], Form(embed=True)] = None,
):
    print(name)
    return name
```

with the following request:
```
❯ http post -v --form localhost:8888 name=""
POST / HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br, zstd
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 5
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Host: localhost:8888
User-Agent: xh/0.24.0

name=

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 2
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Sun, 23 Mar 2025 21:01:49 GMT
Server: uvicorn

""
```
The expected result is that `None` is printed, but instead `""` is printed.

this is the offending bit from the linked PR:
```
diff --git a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py b/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py
index 6083b731..db7eedba 100644
--- a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py
+++ b/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py
@@ -789,9 +789,9 @@ async def _extract_form_body(
             value = serialize_sequence_value(field=field, value=results)
         if value is not None:
             values[field.name] = value
-    for key, value in received_body.items():
-        if key not in values:
-            values[key] = value
+    # for key, value in received_body.items():
+    #     if key not in values:
+    #         values[key] = value
     return values
```
This patch solves the x-form-urlencoded  case. So we indeed have two different regressions.
This _get_multi_dict_value  gets called for all fields in the schema: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L765-L765
we see here the special case for empty fields: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L695-L700
This bit here just blindly re-adds all fields from the body:    https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L794-L796

This would be partially fixed by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12502/files, but also requires that ignored fields are not re-added to the form.


# Breaking in the handling of default value in `multipart` forms

The breaking of multipart form originates from https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12117 where the check for empty field was dropped https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12117/files#diff-aef3dac481b68359f4edd6974fa3a047cfde595254a4567a560cebc9ccb0673fR669

MRE:
```python
app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/")
def root(
     file: Annotated[Optional[bytes], File()] = None,
    name: Annotated[Optional[str], Form(embed=True)] = None,
):
    print(name)
    print(file)
    return name
```

The same request as the previous bug yields name printing an empty string.


Both commits were identified by bisecting using the MREs in this issue

This also solves https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/13255, but scanning through the issues of the repo it seems that there's a bunch of issue related to handling of default values in forms that I suspect may be linked to this issue.
comments: @MarinPostma: You are not specifying the response model.
The response model is inconsequential in this example. I am not interested in the output of the endpoint but in the prints
The issue is with how the request model is being parsed, I have identified the sources of the regressions and have linked them. I am happy to submit a patch that resolves these bugs

Ref.: Breaking in the handling of default value in x-url-encoded forms

*test.py*

```python

from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from main import app


def test_root():
    client = TestClient(app)
    response = client.post("/", data={}) # corrected
    assert response.status_code == 200
```

```command-line

pytest test.py -svv
```

Test result:

```command-line

collected 1 item                                                                                                                                                             

test.py::test_root None
PASSED

```


@MarinPostma: Can you describe how do you pass the `name` value into the form? (I can't reproduce your result.)

Name is passed as an empty field which is a behavior that we observed http clients to have. Inspecting the code we can see that this was handled before by fastapi which coerces empty string value in forms as the absence of the field, later on code was added that overridden this behavior (I linked to the code that does that in the report). From the or descriptions, it seems that this change is not intentional
Also note that it's only for forms, json bodies work as intended

> Also note that it's only for forms, json bodies work as intended

Correction added. :smiley:

It would be good if you could show the code, that you are using with http clients. Maybe the test that was passing before, and that is failing after upgrading to the newer version?
I'm afk right now, I'll prepare a more self contained reproducer for you :)

The gist of it is that some clients handle a null field in a form as an empty form field (rather than the absence thereof) before the pr that I linked, a field that was set to an empty string in fastapi was handled as the absence of such field, which meant that the default value was used instead. After the PRs I have linked, the empty fields are now interpreted as empty strings, so the default value is not used anymore. If you replaced the name, with for example an age of type int, then fastapi would try to parse an int form an empty string and fail. Before, because empty field was interpreted as the absence, then the default value would be used instead.


The code for handling empty string field is still there, it is just inhibited by later code which I linked.

https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/4633b1bca933e68dac5c3bcce797ff5963debe2a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L695-L700

This section of code is the most telling: if the value is an empty string, then return the default value for that field

@alv2017 take the following example, this is the `x-www-form-urlencoded` case:

```python
from typing import Annotated, Optional
import fastapi

app = fastapi.FastAPI()

@app.post("/")
def root(
    age: Annotated[Optional[int], fastapi.Form()] = None,
):
    print(age)
    return age

```

running the following command:
```
curl -v  -X POST http://localhost:8888/ --data-urlencode 'age='
```

with fastapi `0.112.4`

```
Note: Unnecessary use of -X or --request, POST is already inferred.
* Host localhost:8888 was resolved.
* IPv6: ::1
* IPv4: 127.0.0.1
*   Trying [::1]:8888...
* connect to ::1 port 8888 from ::1 port 55215 failed: Connection refused
*   Trying 127.0.0.1:8888...
* Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8888
> POST / HTTP/1.1
> Host: localhost:8888
> User-Agent: curl/8.7.1
> Accept: */*
> Content-Length: 4
> Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
>
* upload completely sent off: 4 bytes
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< date: Mon, 24 Mar 2025 18:57:03 GMT
< server: uvicorn
< content-length: 4
< content-type: application/json
<
* Connection #0 to host localhost left intact
null%
```

with fastapi `0.115.12`:
```
Note: Unnecessary use of -X or --request, POST is already inferred.
* Host localhost:8888 was resolved.
* IPv6: ::1
* IPv4: 127.0.0.1
*   Trying [::1]:8888...
* connect to ::1 port 8888 from ::1 port 55227 failed: Connection refused
*   Trying 127.0.0.1:8888...
* Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8888
> POST / HTTP/1.1
> Host: localhost:8888
> User-Agent: curl/8.7.1
> Accept: */*
> Content-Length: 4
> Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
>
* upload completely sent off: 4 bytes
< HTTP/1.1 422 Unprocessable Entity
< date: Mon, 24 Mar 2025 18:58:03 GMT
< server: uvicorn
< content-length: 145
< content-type: application/json
<
* Connection #0 to host localhost left intact
{"detail":[{"type":"int_parsing","loc":["body","age"],"msg":"Input should be a valid integer, unable to parse string as an integer","input":""}]}%
```

Conclusion, the same request used to work, now, it doesn't work anymore.


Something similar happens with the multi-part content type, but my investigations concluded that these are indeed two separate regressions.
@MarinPostma: thank you for explaining the problem!
My pleasure. I can submit a fix, if this is acknowleged as a bug.
I have opened a PR with a fix. This generally fixes the issue.
wow

The handling of forms also caused [this](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13694) regression, which was also found when migrating from version v0.112.4 to v0.115.11. It would be great to get this merged.
Assign it to me, I'll look over that.
@MarinPostma  can i take over this please?

@MarinPostma @Emmywise 
can we get this fix to latest version also it will help us
I don't think the maintainers have any interest in fixing this unfortunately
Seems like I am a little late, but I also was working on this issue :)

This should have been solved by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13537 (thanks @MarinPostma!).

The fix will be available in FastAPI 0.123.2, released in the next few hours. 🎉

title: HTTPBearer security scheme is returning 403 instead or 401
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9130
Originally posted by **aaaaahaaaaa** September 8, 2020 HTTPBearer security scheme enabled as a dependency is returning a `403` when a request is unauthenticated because of a missing or a malformed `authorization` header. In those scenarios, a `401` should be returned instead.
## Related PRs - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/2120 - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5310 - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5332 - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/9312
comments: I use the `auto_error=False` and control the correct http error code manually.

Hey @Kludex, what do you think about raising a `HTTP 400 BAD REQUEST` for cases where the authentication scheme is not correctly 'bearer'?

[From RFC 6750 Section 3.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6750#section-3.1):

```
invalid_request
         The request is missing a required parameter, includes an
         unsupported parameter or parameter value, repeats the same
         parameter, uses more than one method for including an access
         token, or is otherwise malformed.  The resource server SHOULD
         respond with the HTTP 400 (Bad Request) status code.

invalid_token
         The access token provided is expired, revoked, malformed, or
         invalid for other reasons.  The resource SHOULD respond with
         the HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) status code.  The client MAY
         request a new access token and retry the protected resource
         request.
```

My reading of this text is that:

- when the server understands the request (i.e. 'bearer' scheme is correct) but the token itself is malformed we should return a `401`
- when the server does **not** understand the request (i.e. 'bearer' scheme is wrong), then we should return a `400`
Can you show me the diff you are talking about?

[`fastapi/security/http.py` L#125](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/security/http.py#L125-L130)

EDIT: Wrong line sorry
You want 400 instead of 403? That's why I asked the diff, not the lines in the code source. 👀

Sorry, my misunderstanding. At any rate, I don't think it is necessary after reading section 3 again I realised they offered an example for this scenario.

**tldr; 401 seems like the appropriate error code 👍** 

```
 If the request lacks any authentication information (e.g., the client
   was unaware that authentication is necessary or attempted using an
   unsupported authentication method), the resource server SHOULD NOT
   include an error code or other error information.

   For example:

     HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
     WWW-Authenticate: Bearer realm="example"
```

This not only affects the `HTTPBearer`, but a lot of other classes in the `security` scope also return 403 in `"Not authenticated"` cases where they should return 401 instead, such as the `OAuth2` class (though strangely enough not the subclasses `OAuth2PasswordBearer` and `OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer`), the `HTTPBase` class and its sublasses (except for `HTTPBasic` for some reason), all classes in `api_key.py` and `OpenIdConnect`. Would probably be good if that could be straightened out for all occurrences there.

Will there be any progress in the future? Will this error be fixed? I just hope that when the client receives a 403 response, it prompts the user that they do not have access to the corresponding resource, and when a 401 response is received, it redirects to the login page. It seems that this cannot be achieved with the current default implementation of FastAPI.
For those who are interested in getting this issue resolved, please review #13786
This was handled in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13786, available in FastAPI 0.122.0 🎉
This change broke some of our tests. As a workaround limit the version to `fastapi[all]<0.122.0"` or fix your tests to expect now 401 instead of 403.

> This change broke some of our tests. As a workaround limit the version to `fastapi[all]<0.122.0"` or fix your tests to expect now 401 instead of 403.

Consider also https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/how-to/authentication-error-status-code/

title: Add section to docs about configuration
body:
It would be useful if you could add a section to the (already excellent) docs on the recommended way to add app configuration. Would you recommend something based on Pydantic's BaseSettings, or on Starlette's Config?
Both would work I think, but your opinion on the optimal integration in FastAPI would be useful.
TIA
comments:
Yep, good idea. I have been thinking about adding it.

It's probably gonna be based on Pydantic, it should probably be a bit more powerful, and in FastAPI you are already familiar with it, so it should be relatively easy to use.
Looking forward to it. Thx

fwiw keep testing in mind. For example we want to be able to import `app` into tests for creating a test client but we often want to mock the environment too. This means we want to avoid side-effect of init code firing upon `app` import, such as configuration.

What we currently do on my project:

```py
# lib/config.py
from functools import lru_cache

from pydantic import BaseSettings


class Config(BaseSettings):
    guestlist_client_secret: str
    guestlist_client_id: str
    guestlist_tenant_domain: str
    guestlist_audience: str
    app_name: str = "unknown"
    app_stage: str = "unknown"
    app_place: str = "unknown"
    app_log_level: str = "INFO"
    under_test: bool = False

    class Config:
        env_prefix = ""
        case_insensitive = True


@lru_cache()
def read() -> Config:
    return Config()

```
```py
# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI

from locksmith import lib
from locksmith.routers import healthcheck, sync

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(healthcheck.router)
app.include_router(sync.router)


@app.on_event("startup")
def on_startup():
    config = lib.config.read()
    lib.logging.configure()
```

Furthermore we have routers that need access to config:
```py
# routers/healthcheck.py

...
@router.get("/", response_model=HealthCheck)
def healthcheck():
    log.debug("healthcheck", url="/")
    config = lib.config.read()
    return HealthCheck(
        app=config.app_name, stage=config.app_stage, place=config.app_place, status="OK"
    )
```

It was too much work to get this all setup and it still feels clunky.

Here's a sketch of something that might be better

- wrap `pydantic.BaseSettings` so `env_prefix` defaults to `""` and `case_insensitive` defaults to `True`.
- actually resolve the config at boot time, allowing tests to easily mock the environment
- inject resolved settings it at event and router points

```py
# settings.py
from fastapi import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    app_log_level: str = INFO   # TODO enum
    ...
```
```py
# main.py

from .settings import Settings
from .routers import healthcheck
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
app.include_settings(Settings)
app.include_router(healthcheck.router)

@app.on_event("startup")
def on_startup(settings: Settings):
    lib.logging.configure(log_level=settings.app_log_level)

```
```py
# routers/healthcheck.py

from fastapi import router
from .settings import Settings
...
@router.get("/", response_model=HealthCheck)
def healthcheck(settings:Settings):
    log.debug("healthcheck", url="/")
    return HealthCheck(
        app=settings.app_name,
        stage=settings.app_stage,
        place=settings.app_place,
        status="OK"
    )
```

I was catching up with [dependencies](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/first-steps/) tonight and observed we almost have a great way of handling config already:

```py
# config.py
from fastapi import BaseSettings

class Config(BaseSettings):
    foo: str = "bar"
```
```py
# routers/healthcheck.py
from fastapi import router, Depends
from .config import Config

@router.get("/")
def healthcheck(config:Config = Depends()):
    return dict(
        app=config.app_name,
        stage=config.app_stage,
        place=config.app_place,
        status="OK"
    )
```

But this would require:

1. Depends caching the result of the invoked class 
2. Not allowing the Config constructor as affecting path config

non-fastapi solution to 1 is making `Config` a singleton or bringing back a helper read function using lru_cache

@tiangolo thoughts?

Thanks for all the discussion here!

Yes, we should have docs in the Advanced User Guide about doing app configuration with Pydantic's [`BaseSettings`](https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/settings/).

As it now also supports reading from `.env` files, one simple option that could go in the docs is to have a default in a `.env` file that is suitable for tests with a singleton, and then in production that could be overwritten with env vars.

And we could also have a note saying that depending on the use case, someone could prefer to have those settings generated in a dependency as instead of a singleton.

Nevertheless it would probably work mainly when using env vars only. In the case of `.env` files, getting the config in a dependency AND reading from `.env` files would add a performance penalty as the file would be read from disk for every request. So, to support `.env`, a singleton instance created only at startup would probably be useful for those cases.

---

Another way to solve it for tests would be to have env vars overriding any default in the environment where tests are run. For example, a bash script that creates the env vars and then runs pytest, after having those env vars set.

I also have a case right now where I connect to Firestore database when my application starts. I'd like to mock Firestore in my pytests but I can't figure out how I am supposed to do that. As far as I can tell I can't use .env since I need to monkeypatch an object which is created on startup. Simply changing secrets etc. is not enough. At startup I have

```python
app = FastAPI()
db = MyDB()
```

In my pytest I need to monkeypatch `MyDB`. Any current recommended way to do it?

Maybe I should've put a little more thinking into it before writing. This is my "workaround"

```python
from pydantic import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    LOCAL_DEVELOPMENT = False

    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

app = FastAPI()
settings = Settings()

if settings.LOCAL_DEVELOPMENT:
    from my_package import MockMyDB as MyDB
else:
    from my_package import MyDB

db = MyDB()
```

Great thanks. Looks good. Quick couple of questions though:

1. Having settings as dependency; wouldn’t that disable the initial warm up of the settings when the server start up?
2. would you hold custom types in BaseSettings as well? E.g a custom database class (which could be changed to a mock class in testing)

Yep, that's taken into account :ok_hand:

Check the new docs. :heavy_check_mark: :memo: 

<!-- issue-manager: answered -->
Beautiful (I must’ve missed it)
@tiangolo Thanks for the new docs! Very useful.

Great! @mr-bjerre and @mjmare  :tada: 

If you think this solves your question, feel free to close the issue @mjmare :heavy_check_mark: 

<!-- issue-manager: answered -->
I think this issue can be closed. It would be helpful for newbies to see more info on config strategies with regard to testing vs production. But that might be asked too much for the FastAPI docs.

It's not 100% clear to me when reading the [docs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/settings/#settings-and-testing) how to use settings in testing/mocking when the settings are not used as dependencies. Below an example:

config.py
```py
class Settings(BaseSettings):
    testing: bool = False
    api_key: str = 'some-secret-production-key'

    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

@lru_cache()
def get_settings():
    return Settings()
```

somewhere in the app code i have something like 

```py
from config import get_settings
testing = get_settings().testing
```

Now say I would to mock the settings, in conftest.py I have

```py
@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def mock_settings(mocker): 
    import config
    mocker.patch.object(config, 'get_settings', side_effect=mock_get_settings) 
    mocker.patch('config.get_settings', side_effect=mock_get_settings)
```
where ```mock_get_settings``` overrides the production values with values for testing.

An example test would look like this:

```py
def test_something(client):
    import config
    print(f'{config.get_settings()}')
    from config import get_settings
    print(f'{get_settings()}')
    response = client.get(f"/something/", headers={"ApiKey": get_settings().api_key}) # where the ApiKey is mocked on the call but not in the app :/
    assert response.status_code == status_code
```
The results of the print statements above show testing values mocked as expected but the values are not mocked when ```client.get``` is invoked and the test fails. I would appreciate if someone could give some insight on how to proceed here. Is there something I am doing wrong here?
It's far easier to override the settings when they are a dependency - is there a reason you're choosing not to do it that way?

@mxdev88 You can drop the `@lru_cache` decorator if you are using `get_settings` as a dependency as `Depends` is set to `use_cache=True` by default.(Someone correct me if I'm wrong.) To your question, you can override dependencies before creating a TestClient like so:

```python
app.dependency_overrides[dependency.get_settings] = self.override_settings
```

The thing I haven't been able to figure out is how to override non dependency settings in testing. For example in my instantiation of `FastAPI(title="Fast App", version=settings.version)` in `main.py`, will throw validation errors at the moment Settings() is called. I could just os.getenv(), but would rather to continue to use Pydantic `BaseSettings`. I don't believe Settings() can be mocked either.

Resources: [dependency_overrides](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/testing-dependencies/?h=+dependency+overri#testing-dependencies-with-overrides), [pydantic_basesettings](https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/settings/)

> The thing I haven't been able to figure out is how to override non dependency settings in testing

One of the things I am doing is relying on the behavior of `@lru_cache()` that it always gives me the same physical object. It feels a little hacky but this works pretty well for say, overriding database settings with a test database:

```python
class DatabaseSettings(BaseSettings):
    name: str = "postgres"
    user: str = "postgres"
    password: str = "postgres"
    host: str = "localhost"
    port: int = 5432

    class Config:
        env_prefix = "DB_"

    @property
    def url(self) -> str:
        return f"postgresql://{self.user}:{self.password}@{self.host}:{self.port}/{self.name}"
...
...

@lru_cache()
def get_database_settings() -> DatabaseSettings:
    return DatabaseSettings()
```

And then in my `conftest.py`:

```python
@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def postgres_server() -> Postgresql:
    postgres = testing.postgresql.Postgresql()  # uses testing.postgres dependency to create a brand new database

    postgres_params = postgres.dsn()
    db_settings = get_database_settings()
    db_settings.name = postgres_params["database"]
    db_settings.user = postgres_params["user"]
    db_settings.host = postgres_params["host"]
    db_settings.port = str(postgres_params["port"])

    try:
        wait_for_postgres(str(db_settings.url))  #wait_for_postgres just does retrying
        yield postgres
    finally:
        postgres.stop()
```

I can then make dependencies on that `postgres_server` fixture like running Alembic migrations or initializing an SQLAlchemy database. As long as you define the fixtures correctly, when you execute your test the singleton `DatabaseSettings` object will have been modified by that fixture.

@phillipuniverse Very good suggestion! Breaking settings out of a single large Settings class has resolved my import error issues. I now have 3 classes; 1. Database, 2. Application, and 3. Settings or dependency configurations. This way I'm able to initialize the application and db engine with default values, while overriding my `get_setttings` and `get_db` dependencies in testing. Works like a charm!

title: AttributeError: 'dict' object has no attribute 'split'
body:
Minimum Reproducible Example:

```py
# /// script
# dependencies = [
#   "pydantic",
#   "fastapi[standard]",
#   "starlette",
# ]
# ///

import pydantic
import starlette
import fastapi
from fastapi import FastAPI
import sys
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ConfigDict

# print the dependencies to stdout, one per line
print(f'pydantic version = {pydantic.__version__}')
print(f'starlette version = {starlette.__version__}')
print(f'fastapi version = {fastapi.__version__}')
# print python version
print(f'Python version = {sys.version}')


app = FastAPI(title='Logfire')

class ModelWithRef(BaseModel):
```

title: `Depends(func, scope="function")` with `yield` dependency exits after the request is sent instead of after the function
body:
Adding this issue for completeness. Solved by: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14301 ### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/14296
Originally posted by **guldfisk** November 5, 2025 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from types import NoneType from typing import Iterator, Annotated from fastapi import APIRouter from fastapi import Depends from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() class ApplicationError(Exception): ... def dependency_with_cleanup_error() -> Iterator[None]: yield raise ApplicationError() router = APIRouter( dependencies=[Depends(dependency_with_cleanup_error, scope="function")] ) @router.get("/broken") def broken(): # Will return 200, and then fail in the cleanup. return {"status": "ok"} @router.get("/works") def endpoint( v: Annotated[NoneType, Depends(dependency_with_cleanup_error, scope="function")], ): # Will correctly return 500 return {"status": "ok"} app.include_router(router) ``` ### Description When using the new "scope" feature for yield dependencies, function scope is still cleaned up after the response is sent, if the dependency is requested in the api router, instead of on the path function. Maybe this is a feature, as in "function" should be interpreted as the scope of where the dependency is requested, and not always the path function, but I can't find any documentation mentioning this behavior, and in any case I'm not sure what the usecase for that would be. ### Operating System Linux ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.121.0 ### Pydantic Version 2.12.3 ### Python Version 3.11.12 ### Additional Context _No response_
comments: This was solved by https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14301, released in FastAPI 0.121.1. 🐛

title: CORS Middleware and exception handler
AssertionError: fastapi_inner_astack not found in request scope
body:
### Privileged issue

- [x] I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

### Issue Content

latest version of Fastapi is not support with Starlette 0.48.0.

FAFastAPI 0.118.0
Starlette 0.48.0
comments:
Not sure if it's the same issue, but we are seeing the similar "AssertionError: fastapi_middleware_astack not found in request scope
" in https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/actions/runs/18114950033/job/51548802086 since the release of fastapi 0.118.0
Do you have an MRE?

To be able to help you, we would need a minimal reproducible example and all the information that we ask in a GitHub Discussion question.

That's why there's a check box for:

> I'm @tiangolo or he asked me directly to create an issue here.

Please, create a GitHub Discussion question with your specific use case and a minimal reproducible example and we'll be able to understand your use case and help you: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/new?category=questions

For now, I'll close this one. ☕

> Do you have an MRE?

You can run pytest of https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/blob/dev/test/unit/webapps/api/test_cbv.py where cbv.py is https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/blob/dev/lib/galaxy/webapps/galaxy/api/cbv.py

This works fine under fastapi 0.117.0 and fails under 0.118.0 with:

```
equest = <starlette.requests.Request object at 0x7763eace1450>

    async def app(request: Request) -> Response:
        response: Union[Response, None] = None
        file_stack = request.scope.get("fastapi_middleware_astack")
>       assert isinstance(file_stack, AsyncExitStack), (
            "fastapi_middleware_astack not found in request scope"
        )
E       AssertionError: fastapi_middleware_astack not found in request scope
```
I need an MRE to help.
Is this better? https://gist.github.com/nsoranzo/75be3a2ac682802bed71bfc18c99aef6

Ah, I see. The problem is that FastAPI doesn't run the `build_middleware_stack` for an `APIRouter`.

If in your test, you use the router from the `FastAPI` object instead, your test will pass.

From 0.118.0, it seems FastAPI doesn't work in "ASGI blocks" anymore.

btw, this is an MRE:
```py
from fastapi import APIRouter
from starlette.testclient import TestClient


router = APIRouter()


@router.get("/items")
async def root():
    return "hi"


client = TestClient(router)
client.get("/items")
```

Thanks @Kludex !

This fixes the above MRE:

```diff
--- mre.py	2025-09-30 12:50:30.605246667 +0100
+++ mre_fixed.py	2025-09-30 13:19:36.406692058 +0100
@@ -1,4 +1,7 @@
-from fastapi import APIRouter
+from fastapi import (
+    APIRouter,
+    FastAPI
+)
 from starlette.testclient import TestClient
 
 
@@ -10,5 +13,7 @@
     return "hi"
 
 
-client = TestClient(router)
+app = FastAPI()
+app.include_router(router)
+client = TestClient(app)
 client.get("/items")
```

> this error ocurrs when paginating resuts:

Please, report this in the repository of `fastapi_pagination` package that you are using

> [@tiangolo](https://github.com/tiangolo) [@Kludex](https://github.com/Kludex) hello, this error ocurrs when paginating resuts:
> 
> from fastapi_pagination import Page from fastapi_pagination.ext.sqlalchemy import paginate
> 
> @contents_router.get( "", response_model=Page[UploadedContentResponse], status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK, tags=["Contents"], )
> 
> Is there any solution for this issue?

Just in case someone else stumbles upon this error - it's solved with `fastapi-pagination` v0.14.2 [[link](https://github.com/uriyyo/fastapi-pagination/issues/1599)]

body:

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Description

* For contributing translations, it helps that tools or notification to Inform us out of dates translations.
* (I believe) translated documents should shows if up-to-date or out-of-dates to original document. it help users.

### Wanted Solution

First of all, to solve how to search out-of-date translations, I created simple tools ([PR](#5641)) to compare committed date between original and translated docs. It works (but not correct).

How about merging the tools and collaborate them to notify it for developer and user with something like GitHub Actions?

### Alternatives

* adding revision number in each documents and parse it for developer. this way has advantages as for user to check if up-to-date or not when reading.

### Additional Context

_No response_
comments:
> (I believe) translated documents should shows if up-to-date or out-of-dates to original document. it help users.

Agree! Also, it could be misleading, if there's no such an information.
I'm not sure if in the form you suggested, but It would be great to have such a functionality.

> I created simple tools ([PR](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/5642#5641)) to compare committed date between original and translated docs

Can you share the link to those tools with us? It seems that the link in the "PR" is wrong.

Also, it seems to me that it's more of a MkDocs localization problem than a FastAPI's.

From what I could gather, there's no official way to handle outdated translations in MkDocs or ReadTheDocs 😬.

title: Component name regression in OpenAPI spec for v0.119.0
body:
Adding the issue here for completeness, although most of the conversation is in the GitHub Discussion. ### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/14177
Originally posted by **walsha2** October 12, 2025 ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title here. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from enum import StrEnum from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import BaseModel, Field class MessageEventType(StrEnum): alpha = "alpha" beta = "beta" class MessageEvent(BaseModel): event_type: MessageEventType = Field(default=MessageEventType.alpha) output: str class MessageOutput(BaseModel): body: str = "" events: list[MessageEvent] class Message(BaseModel): id: str input: str output: MessageOutput app = FastAPI(title="Minimal FastAPI App", version="1.0.0") @app.post("/messages", response_model=Message) async def create_message(input_message: str) -> Message: return Message( input=input_message, output=MessageOutput(body=f"Processed: {input_message}"), ) if __name__ == "__main__": openapi_spec = app.openapi() for c in list(openapi_spec.get("components", {}).get("schemas", {}).keys()): print(c) ``` ### Description ## Issue Summary Take the minimal example provided above, run with `fastapi==0.118.3` and it will produce: ``` HTTPValidationError Message MessageEvent MessageEventType MessageOutput ValidationError ``` Now run the same code with `fastapi==0.119.0` and it will produce: ``` HTTPValidationError Message MessageEvent MessageEventType MessageOutput-Input MessageOutput-Output ValidationError ``` Note how `MessageOutput` is now suffixed unnecessarily as: `MessageOutput-Input` and `MessageOutput-Output`. This seems unexpected considering the changes made between `v0.118.3` and `v0.119.0` ## Minor Tweak One minor tweak to the example and this issue goes away. Remove the enum from `MessageEvent`: ```python class MessageEvent(BaseModel): output: str ``` Run again and the issue is no longer there. ``` HTTPValidationError Message MessageEvent MessageOutput ValidationError ``` ## TLDR Why does the presence of an enum value on `MessageEvent` create a bifurcated OpenAPI schema definition for `MessageOutput` and why does this issue only start happening in `fastapi==0.119.0`? ### Operating System macOS ### Operating System Details 15.6.1 (24G90) ### FastAPI Version 0.119.0 ### Pydantic Version 2.12.0 ### Python Version Python 3.13.1 ### Additional Context _No response_
comments: It should be fixed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14246, just relased in FastAPI 0.120.2. :tada:
